1: Chapter 5 is the first of three extended warnings against the 'foreign/strange woman' (Proverbs 5, Proverbs 6:20-35, 7). The genre corresponds to warnings in Egyptian wisdom: the Instruction of Any warns, 'be on your guard against a woman from abroad, who is not known in her town.' The ANE concern is broadly shared: the foreign woman represents danger to the household and its economic stability.
2: Discretion (mĕzimmôt) and knowledge (da'at) are the specific wisdom-qualities threatened by the foreign woman. The wisdom school's purpose is to produce the kind of discernment that identifies and resists the seductive speech described in v3; this chapter can be read as a practical wisdom-school exercise in applied discrimination.
3: The 'strange/foreign woman' (zārāh) is one of the most debated figures in Proverbs scholarship. Three interpretations have been offered: (1) a literal foreign woman (representing the apostasy danger of Deuteronomy 7:3-4); (2) a prostitute or adulteress (the most common reading); (3) a metaphor for Lady Folly, the foil to Lady Wisdom of Proverbs 8-9. All three may be simultaneously in view: the text is deliberately multi-layered.
4: The bitter-end contrast with the honeyed beginning is a well-established wisdom teaching on deceptive appearances. The Egyptian Instruction of Ankhsheshonq: 'Beautiful is a woman whose words are sweet, but her interior is bitter poison.' The wisdom tradition across the ANE recognized that pleasure leading to destruction is more dangerous than direct adversity.
5: Her feet going down to death and Sheol connects to the underworld geography of the ancient Near East: the dead descend to the realm below. Proverbs 2:18-19 makes the same point; 7:27 climaxes it: 'her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death.' The foreign woman is mapped onto the descent-to-death spatial imagery of the underworld.
6: She does not ponder the path of life — the peles (weighing/balance) imagery links her to the section's broader concern with measured, examined paths (Proverbs 4:26). The chaotic, unmeasured quality of her paths is the opposite of the wise person's deliberate, examined way of living.
7: The father returns to the plural 'sons' (as in Proverbs 4:1) — signaling either a school-address or a deliberate universalizing of the warning. The instruction is not private but communal; wisdom is transmitted through communities of practice, not solitary learning.
8: Distance rather than resistance — 'keep your way far from her' — is the wisdom strategy. Compare Joseph's flight from Potiphar's wife (Genesis 39:12: 'he left his garment in her hand and fled and got out of the house'). Physical distance is the wisest first response to sexual temptation; the wisdom tradition consistently commends avoidance over confrontation.
9: Honor, years, and strength given to the 'others' — the economic devastation of adultery connects to the broader wisdom concern with household management. Proverbs 6:26 returns to the cost-accounting: the adultery transaction costs a man's precious life. The wisdom tradition treats sexual fidelity as integral to economic stewardship.
10: The 'strangers' (zārîm) who benefit from the adulterer's loss are the very 'foreignness' the word zārāh (strange woman) connotes: by going to the foreign woman, the fool enriches foreigners with what should have stayed in the household. The word-play reinforces the structural irony.
11: The groaning-at-the-end (v11-14) is a wisdom genre: the retrospective regret speech that inverts the initial seduction. This genre teaches through negative example: by imagining the speaker's regret, the student is warned not to take the path that leads there. Ben Sira (Sirach Proverbs 5:7-8; Proverbs 18:24-25) develops the same genre of prospective regret-avoidance.
12: 'I hated discipline' — the retrospective voice identifies the root of the failure: not weakness in the face of temptation but the prior rejection of correction. Proverbs 15:32: 'whoever ignores instruction despises himself.' The wisdom tradition consistently treats the rejection of correction as the primary cause of moral catastrophe.
13: The teachers (môrāy) and instructors (mĕlamĕdāy) of v13 confirm the school-context of Proverbs' wisdom transmission. The regret is social as well as personal: the failed student brings shame to the community of teachers who invested in him. Ben Sira Proverbs 11:28: 'call no one blessed before his death; a man is known by how he ends.'
14: 'In the midst of the assembly and congregation' — the communal setting of disgrace connects to the covenant assembly (qāhāl, 'ēdāh). These are the terms used for Israel's covenant gathering (Numbers 16:3; Deuteronomy 9:10; Proverbs 31:30); to be shamed 'in the congregation' is to be exposed before the covenant community. Sexual dishonor has public, covenantal dimensions in Israel.
15: 'Drink water from your own cistern' — the source-imagery for the marriage relationship has ANE parallels. The Egyptian love poetry of the Chester Beatty papyri uses water and garden imagery for the beloved. More directly, Song of Solomon 4:12-15 uses the 'locked garden,' 'sealed fountain,' and 'springs of water' for the beloved wife — imagery structurally parallel to Proverbs 5:15-18. Both texts draw on a shared reservoir of ANE erotic imagery.
16: The rhetorical question about springs going public (ḥûṣāh = outside/in the streets) addresses the logic of fidelity: the water that belongs to the household does not spread to public channels. The wisdom tradition treats marital sexuality as household-proprietary — not merely personal preference but economic and social boundary-maintenance.
17: 'For you alone' (lĕbaddĕkā) — the exclusivity principle that grounds marriage covenant. The covenant vocabulary of exclusivity — 'you shall have no other gods before me' (Exodus 20:3) — has its domestic analogue in marital fidelity. The Hosea-YHWH/Israel marriage metaphor (Hos 1-3) shows how thoroughly the OT interweaves covenant and marriage logic.
18: 'Wife of your youth' (ēšet nĕ'ûreḵā) — the youth-marriage ideal appears also in Malachi 2:14-15: 'the LORD was witness between you and the wife of your youth... she is your companion and the wife of your covenant.' Malachi's condemnation of divorce uses the same vocabulary; both Proverbs and Malachi ground marital fidelity in the original covenant commitment of youth.
19: The explicit erotic language of v19 (breasts, satisfaction, ravished) stands in the tradition of explicit sexual celebration in Song of Solomon. The wisdom tradition does not spiritualize away the physical dimension of marriage. The canon's inclusion of both Song of Solomon and Proverbs 5 affirms that sexual joy within the covenant relationship is a positive good that wisdom commends rather than merely tolerates.
20: 'Why be intoxicated (šāgāh) with a foreign woman?' — the intoxication/wandering verb connects to the description of the drunkard's unstable gait in Proverbs 23:33. Sexual infatuation and drunkenness share the same disorienting effect in wisdom's analysis: both produce wandering, stumbling, and loss of moral direction.
21: 'For a man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD' — the divine surveillance theme has canonical parallels in Psalms 139:1-4 (YHWH knows every path and thought), Job 31:4 (God watches all my steps and counts my journeys), and Jeremiah 16:17 (YHWH's eyes are on all their ways). The omniscient divine gaze is one of wisdom's primary deterrents: nothing is private before YHWH.
22: 'His own iniquities will trap him' — the self-entrapment principle is emphasized in the prophets: Isaiah 5:18 ('woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood'); Hosea 5:4 ('their deeds do not permit them to return to their God; for the spirit of whoredom is within them, and they know not the LORD'). Sin creates a structural bondage that removes freedom of action over time.
23: 'He will die for lack of discipline' — the fatal consequence of rejected wisdom forms an inclusio with v12 ('I hated discipline'). The chapter demonstrates Proverbs' central logic: wisdom offers life; its rejection leads to death. The same binary appears in Deuteronomy 30:19 ('I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse; therefore choose life').
